pathophysiology_summary: Pityriasis rosea is a benign, self-limiting exanthem (associated with HHV-6/7 reactivation) that typically begins with a solitary 'herald patch' followed 1–2 weeks later by a symmetrical eruption of oval, salmon-coloured, finely-scaling patches distributed along skin cleavage lines (a 'Christmas-tree' pattern on the back), each with a characteristic inward-facing 'collarette' scale, sparing the face, palms and soles; there is often a preceding viral prodrome and mild itch. It resolves spontaneously over 6–8 weeks and is not contagious. The ground-truth task is to recognise the classic pattern and, importantly, to exclude the key mimic — secondary syphilis (which characteristically involves the palms and soles, may have mucosal lesions/lymphadenopathy and a relevant sexual history) — as well as a drug eruption, guttate psoriasis and widespread tinea. Management is reassurance, gentle skin care/emollients and an antihistamine for itch.

differential.evidence_basis: Herald patch, Christmas-tree oval patches with collarette scale after a viral prodrome, sparing face/palms/soles.
The great mimic — screen palm/sole involvement, mucosal lesions and sexual history; would need serology/treatment.
Thick silvery scale and no herald patch/collarette — argues against.
Annular expanding rings (tinea) or a new-drug history (eruption) — not the pattern here.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Pityriasis rosea leads; secondary syphilis must be actively excluded (palms/soles/mucosa/sexual history) before reassurance.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic pattern with no palm/sole or mucosal involvement and no new drug — benign pityriasis rosea.
No palm/sole/mucosal involvement; escalation/serology if palms/soles or mucosa become involved or risk history emerges.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative syphilis screen, pityriasis rosea leads; reassurance + gentle skin care + antihistamine.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis; self-limiting over 6–8 weeks, non-contagious.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final position is benign pityriasis rosea.

red_flags.description: Secondary syphilis features — rash on the palms/soles, mucosal (oral/genital) lesions, lymphadenopathy, or relevant sexual-risk history
red_flags.clinical_significance: Secondary syphilis mimics pityriasis rosea but is a treatable systemic infection needing serology and antibiotics.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not checking palms/soles/mucosa and sexual history — the classic pityriasis-rosea mimic.
Aggressive washing dries and irritates the skin, worsening the itch.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassurance + gentle skin care + antihistamine; arrange syphilis serology / review if palms/soles or mucosa become involved or risk history is present.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Pityriasis rosea is benign and self-limiting; manage with reassurance, emollients and antihistamines for itch. Consider secondary syphilis if palms/soles are involved or there is a relevant history — check serology.
